title: Dysphania × bontei
intro: Dysphania × bontei, also known as Good King Henry, is a biennial or perennial plant that belongs to the Amaranthaceae family. It has been used in traditional medicine for its health benefits, including its potential to reduce inflammation, improve digestion, and exhibit antioxidant properties, making it a popular remedy for various ailments. The bioactive constituents of Good King Henry include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and other antioxidant compounds, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Good King Henry include teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are used to treat conditions such as arthritis, gastrointestinal issues, and skin problems.
taxonomy: Dysphania × bontei is a hybrid species of flowering plant in the family Amaranthaceae. It is a cross between Dysphania ambrosioides and Dysphania pumilio. As a hybrid species, it is not assigned a distinct classification rank, but its parent species are classified as belonging to the genus Dysphania. The classification of Dysphania × bontei is still evolving, and its classification is subject to ongoing research. Dysphania × bontei is often treated as a synonym of its parent species.
uses: Dysphania × bontei, also known as Bonté's bittercress, has been used in traditional medicine for its potential health benefits. Its leaves and stems are rich in antioxidants and have been studied for their anti-inflammatory properties. In some cultures, it has been used to treat digestive issues and reduce fever. Additionally, the plant has been used in traditional remedies for its antimicrobial and antiviral properties. More research is needed to fully understand the medicinal uses of Dysphania × bontei.
